content: Linear regression collects all features into a vector x and all weights into a vector w, expressing the prediction compactly as the dot product between w and x. For an entire dataset, the features are represented by the design matrix X, with one row for every example and one column for every feature, and predictions are expressed as y_hat = Xw with broadcasting for the bias. Even when the true relationship is believed to be linear, D2L includes a noise term because measurements of features and labels may contain error. The model needs a loss function and a procedure for updating parameters. The analytic solution for linear regression requires X^T X to be invertible, which means the columns of the design matrix are linearly independent. Minibatch stochastic gradient descent updates parameters by sampling random minibatches and moving in the negative gradient direction, whereas full gradient descent computes derivatives over the entire dataset before an update. Vectorized computation processes whole minibatches with optimized linear algebra libraries and avoids slow Python loops. Squared loss can be motivated probabilistically by assuming additive zero-mean Gaussian noise; under fixed variance, minimizing mean squared error is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation.

Softmax regression handles classification by using multiple outputs, one for each class, so that the model can estimate conditional probabilities for all possible classes. In the D2L example with four input features and three classes, the model uses three affine functions to produce three raw scores or logits. The softmax function exponentiates these raw outputs and normalizes them so the resulting class probabilities are nonnegative and sum to one. Softmax preserves the ordering of its arguments, so the largest logit is also the class with the largest softmax probability. D2L derives the softmax cross-entropy gradient and explains that the derivative is the difference between the probability assigned by the model and the one-hot label representing what actually happened, analogous to regression gradients comparing an estimate with an observation.

D2L motivates multilayer perceptrons by explaining the limitations of linear models. Linearity implies monotonicity: increasing a feature must always move the model output in one direction depending on the sign of its weight. Many real relationships are nonlinear or non-monotonic, so hidden layers are introduced. An MLP stacks fully connected layers, where earlier layers learn representations and the final layer acts as a linear predictor. However, stacking affine transformations alone does not help, because an affine function of an affine function is still affine and can collapse into a single equivalent linear layer. To realize the potential of multilayer architectures, nonlinear activation functions must be inserted between affine layers. ReLU, the rectified linear unit, returns max(x, 0), preserving positive inputs and setting negative ones to zero. D2L highlights ReLU's simplicity, good performance, and well-behaved derivatives, which help mitigate vanishing gradients. Sigmoid activations map real inputs to (0, 1) but can suffer from gradients that vanish for large positive or negative inputs.

D2L presents Xavier initialization as a practical method for stabilizing neural network training. It reasons about keeping activation and gradient variances from exploding or vanishing by balancing the number of inputs and outputs of a layer, often called fan-in and fan-out. Dropout is introduced as another regularization tool for avoiding overfitting. During training, standard dropout randomly zeros out a fraction of activations and rescales the retained activations by the retention probability so that the expected activation remains unchanged. In practice, dropout is usually disabled at test time; the model does not drop nodes and does not need the same normalization. High-level APIs implement dropout layers that randomly drop outputs during training and pass data through unchanged in evaluation mode.

In convolutional neural networks, kernels larger than one pixel can shrink feature maps after repeated convolutions, causing boundary information to be lost. Padding solves this by adding filler pixels around the boundary of the input. Padding is often chosen so that the output height and width match the input height and width, making layer shapes easier to predict and ensuring boundary pixels are used more evenly. Stride is the number of rows and columns the convolution window moves on each slide. A stride larger than one skips intermediate locations, which can improve computational efficiency or intentionally downsample the representation. D2L notes that larger stride can reduce output resolution, such as halving height and width when stride is two.

D2L compares search strategies for sequence decoding. Greedy search selects the token with the highest conditional probability at each time step, which is computationally cheap but is not guaranteed to produce the most likely complete sequence. Exhaustive search would enumerate all possible output sequences and choose the one with the highest probability, but its cost is exponential in sequence length and vocabulary size. Beam search is a compromise between greedy and exhaustive search. With beam size k, it keeps the top candidate sequences at each time step and expands those candidates, providing better search than greedy decoding without the prohibitive cost of exhaustive enumeration. Greedy search is a special case of beam search when beam size is one.

section_title: 11.1-11.2 Queries, Keys, Values, and Attention Pooling
content: The attention mechanism aggregates data from key-value pairs in response to a query. It computes attention weights from the compatibility between a query and keys, then uses those weights to form a weighted combination over values. When weights are nonnegative and sum to one, attention can be interpreted as a convex combination of values. If exactly one weight is one and all others are zero, attention resembles a traditional database query. If all weights are equal, it becomes average pooling. D2L describes attention as differentiable control that lets a neural network select elements from a set and construct a weighted sum over representations. Kernel-based attention pooling by similarity, such as Gaussian, boxcar, or Epanechikov kernels, gives different notions of range and smoothness.

Multi-head attention lets a model jointly use different representation subspaces of queries, keys, and values. Instead of performing a single attention pooling operation, queries, keys, and values are independently transformed through learned linear projections and fed into multiple attention heads in parallel. The outputs of those heads are concatenated and transformed through another learned linear projection. D2L explains that each head may attend to different parts of the input, allowing more sophisticated functions than a simple weighted average. Parallel tensor manipulation is used so multiple heads can be computed efficiently.

In self-attention, the queries, keys, and values all come from the same input sequence. Each token can directly attend to every other token, giving self-attention the shortest maximum path length and allowing parallel computation. Compared with RNNs, it avoids sequential operations; compared with CNNs, it directly connects all token positions. The drawback is quadratic computational complexity with respect to sequence length, making self-attention prohibitively slow for very long sequences. Because self-attention alone does not preserve the order of tokens, positional encodings are added to input representations to inject absolute or relative order information while retaining parallel computation.

The Transformer architecture is based solely on attention mechanisms without convolutional or recurrent layers. D2L describes it as an encoder-decoder model whose source and target embeddings are combined with positional encodings before entering stacks of modules based on self-attention. Each Transformer encoder layer contains multi-head self-attention pooling and a positionwise feed-forward network, along with residual connections and normalization. Transformers were originally proposed for sequence-to-sequence text learning but became pervasive across language, vision, speech, and reinforcement learning applications.

D2L describes BERT pretraining using examples generated from the WikiText-2 dataset. The original BERT models include a base version with 12 Transformer encoder layers, 768 hidden units, and 12 self-attention heads, and a large version with 24 layers, 1024 hidden units, and 16 self-attention heads. For a batch of pretraining examples, D2L computes loss for both masked language modeling and next sentence prediction. The final BERT pretraining loss is the sum of the masked language modeling loss and the next sentence prediction loss.

For Gaussian process regression, D2L assumes a Gaussian process prior over a latent noise-free function with a mean function and a kernel function, plus additive zero-mean Gaussian noise. It describes a two-step procedure: first learn kernel hyperparameters by maximizing the marginal likelihood, then use the predictive mean as a point predictor and two times the predictive standard deviation to form a 95 percent credible set. The predictive mean depends explicitly on the target values, while the predictive variance depends on test locations, training locations, and the kernel. Major computational bottlenecks are solving a linear system and computing a log determinant over an n by n symmetric positive definite kernel matrix; naively these require O(n^3) computation and O(n^2) storage, often via Cholesky decomposition.
